seo.title: Rayong Province, Thailand — Complete Guide 2026
seo.metaDescription: Everything about Rayong Province: Koh Samet island, Mae Ramphueng Beach, fruit orchards, fish sauce production, and 8 districts. Area: 3,552 km², population: 821,000. Plan your visit.

overview: Rayong Province sits on Thailand's Eastern Seaboard along the Gulf of Thailand, roughly 179 km southeast of Bangkok. Part of the Eastern Economic Corridor (EEC), it blends industrial powerhouse with natural beauty — oil refineries and petrochemical plants share the coastline with white-sand beaches and tropical fruit orchards. The province is famous for Koh Samet (a national park island a 40-minute ferry from Ban Phe pier), the 12-km Mae Ramphueng Beach, and being the birthplace of Thailand's celebrated poet Sunthorn Phu. Rayong is also the heart of Thailand's fish sauce (nam pla) industry and produces some of the country's finest durian, rambutan, and mangosteen.
geography: Rayong covers 3,552 km² along the northern shore of the Gulf of Thailand. The terrain is largely flat coastal plain in the south, rising to forested hills and the Khao Chamao–Khao Wong mountain range in the north. The province has a long coastline with several islands of which Koh Samet — within the Khao Laem Ya–Mu Ko Samet National Park — is the most visited. Rivers include the Rayong River that flows through the capital city. The province borders Chonburi to the west and north, and Chanthaburi to the east.
highlights: Koh Samet — a national park island with powder-white beaches just 2 hours from Bangkok
Mae Ramphueng Beach — a 12-km undeveloped coastline with pine tree shade, part of a national park
Khao Laem Ya–Mu Ko Samet National Park — crystal-clear waters, coral reefs, and jungle-clad hills
Tropical fruit orchards — durian, rambutan, mangosteen, and longan fruit buffets (April–July)
Sunthorn Phu Memorial — honoring Thailand's most revered poet, born in Rayong in 1786
Khao Chamao–Khao Wong National Park — 8-tier waterfall and limestone cave trekking
Fish sauce (nam pla) production — Rayong is the national capital of Thailand's premium fish sauce industry
Ban Phe fishing village — fresh seafood markets and ferry gateway to Koh Samet

bestTimeToVisit.peak: Nov–Apr: Dry season with calm seas, clear skies, and temperatures of 25–33°C. Best conditions for Koh Samet and beach activities. December and January are the coolest and most comfortable months. High tourist numbers on Koh Samet at weekends.
bestTimeToVisit.shoulder: Oct and May: Transitional months with occasional showers but generally manageable. Sea conditions can be choppy on some days. Fruit orchards begin producing from May.
bestTimeToVisit.lowSeason: Jun–Sep: Southwest monsoon brings regular heavy rain and rougher seas. Koh Samet stays relatively dry (it sits in a rain shadow) compared to the rest of eastern Thailand, making it the best beach option in the region during the wet season. Hotel prices drop 20–35% on the mainland.
bestTimeToVisit.recommendation: Visit November to April for the best weather and calmest seas. Koh Samet is one of Thailand's most reliable year-round beach destinations due to its rain-shadow position — even in the wet season it receives less rainfall than the Gulf's west coast.

topAttractions.name: Koh Samet (Ko Samet)
topAttractions.description: A national park island 8 km off the Ban Phe coast, famous for its brilliantly white quartz-sand beaches and clear turquoise water. The most popular beaches are Hat Sai Kaew (Diamond Beach), Ao Phai, and Ao Cho. The island has a car-free interior and offers snorkeling, kayaking, and a lively beachfront dining and bar scene. Being just 2 hours from Bangkok, it draws large weekend crowds — arrive midweek for a quieter experience.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB adults, 100 THB children (Thai nationals: 40 THB). Fee valid for 5 days. Ferry from Ban Phe pier: ~80 THB one way

topAttractions.name: Mae Ramphueng Beach
topAttractions.description: Rayong's longest and most natural mainland beach, stretching 12 km along the coast near Ban Phe. Pine trees shade the sand and provide natural windbreaks. Far less developed than Pattaya, it has seafood restaurants, sunbeds for hire, and calm shallow water suitable for families. Part of Khao Laem Ya National Park, making it one of the cleanest beaches in eastern Thailand.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 40 THB adults, free for children. Cars: 30 THB

topAttractions.name: Khao Chamao–Khao Wong National Park
topAttractions.description: A 83-sq-km forested national park straddling Rayong and Chanthaburi provinces. The centerpiece is Khao Chamao Waterfall — an 8-tier cascade descending more than 3 km through jungle, hikeable to the summit in around 2 hours. Khao Wong Cave and Klong Pla Kang Waterfall are other highlights. Rich in wildlife including gibbons, hornbills, and Asian elephants.
topAttractions.entranceFee: 200 THB adults, 100 THB children (Thai nationals: 40 THB / 20 THB)

topAttractions.name: Sunthorn Phu Memorial Park
topAttractions.description: A scenic park commemorating Sunthorn Phu (1786–1855), widely regarded as Thailand's greatest poet. Born in Klaeng district, Rayong, he wrote the epic poem Phra Aphai Mani. The park features a large statue of the poet, gardens, and exhibits about his life and works. Located near Klaeng town, it is a pilgrimage site for Thai literature enthusiasts.

topAttractions.name: Ban Phe Seafood Market
topAttractions.description: The bustling fishing pier town that serves as the gateway to Koh Samet. The market is lined with stalls selling ultra-fresh catch — Rayong mantis shrimp (kung kra pao), squid, sea bass, and blue crab at prices far below Bangkok. Ferries to Koh Samet depart from the adjacent Nuan Thip and Saphan Sri Ban Phe piers.

topAttractions.name: Rayong Fruit Orchards
topAttractions.description: Rayong is one of Thailand's premier tropical fruit-growing regions, famed for its Mon Thong durian, rambutan, mangosteen, and longan. From April to late July, dozens of orchards open for 'fruit buffet' experiences where visitors pay a fixed rate to eat unlimited fresh fruit direct from the trees. Several orchards near Ban Phe and Klaeng offer guided tours.
topAttractions.entranceFee: Varies by orchard — typically 150–300 THB per person for fruit buffet (seasonal, Apr–Jul)

localFood: Nam Pla Rayong (fish sauce) — Rayong is Thailand's fish sauce capital; local brands aged 12–18 months from small marine fish are sold at markets and make an iconic souvenir
Kung Kra Pao (mantis shrimp) — the native Thai mantis shrimp from Rayong waters, served in bami noodle soup or stir-fried with garlic at Ban Phe seafood stalls
Sen Klaeng Pad Tha Le — Klaeng-style flat rice noodles stir-fried with fresh local seafood, a Rayong dish rarely found elsewhere
Mon Thong Durian — Rayong grows some of Thailand's finest Mon Thong durian, harvested May–July; sold at roadside stalls along Highway 36
Kapi (shrimp paste) — house-made fermented shrimp paste from Ban Phe fishing communities, used as a base ingredient throughout eastern Thai cooking
Pla Kapong Neung Manao (steamed sea bass with lime) — fresh-caught sea bass from the Gulf, steamed in a hot broth with chili, lime, and garlic
Khao Yam Pak Tai — southern-style rice salad with aromatic herbs, dried shrimp, and kapi dressing, popular at local markets
Fresh fruit buffet — rambutan, mangosteen, longan, and durian eaten direct from orchards during the April–July harvest season
culture: Rayong has a strong identity rooted in its fishing heritage and literary tradition. The province's most celebrated figure is poet Sunthorn Phu, whose epic tales of sea voyages mirror Rayong's coastal soul. The annual Sunthorn Phu Day (June 26) is commemorated with cultural performances in Klaeng. Buddhism is central to daily life with ornate temples dotting every district. The Eastern Seaboard's industrial boom has brought a large population of migrant workers from Isaan and neighboring countries, giving Rayong's food scene a broad range of regional Thai cuisines alongside local seafood traditions.
gettingThere: From Bangkok, the most popular option is an air-conditioned bus from Ekkamai (Eastern Bus Terminal) or Mo Chit, operated by Rayong Tour and BH Rayong — buses run frequently throughout the day, taking approximately 3–4 hours and costing 120–180 THB. Shared minivans depart from Victory Monument and Ekkamai, offering faster door-to-door service in around 2 hours for roughly 150 THB. By car from Bangkok via Highway 7 (motorway) takes about 2 hours (179 km). There is no direct airport serving Rayong; the nearest international airport is U-Tapao (Rayong–Pattaya Airport, UTP) in the adjacent Chonburi/Rayong border area, served by Bangkok Airways and budget carriers, approximately 30 minutes from Rayong city.
gettingAround: Songthaew (shared pickup trucks) are the main local transport around Rayong city and between towns, running fixed routes for 15–40 THB. To reach Koh Samet, take any bus or songthaew to Ban Phe pier (30 minutes from Rayong city), then a 20–40 minute ferry. Renting a motorbike is the best way to explore Koh Samet island (200–300 THB/day). On the mainland, Grab is available in Rayong city. Private taxis can be hired for day trips to Khao Chamao National Park (approx. 1.5 hours north of the city) or to fruit orchards. A car or hired driver is recommended for exploring multiple sites in one day.
travelTips: Book Koh Samet accommodation well in advance for Thai long weekends — the island fills completely and ferries can sell out
Koh Samet sits in a geographic rain shadow and receives significantly less rainfall than the Thai mainland — a good wet-season beach backup
Buy Rayong fish sauce and kapi (shrimp paste) at Ban Phe market as souvenirs — factory brands cost a fraction of Bangkok prices
The durian season (May–July) is the best time to visit Rayong's orchards; Mon Thong variety from Rayong is considered premium quality
Ferries to Koh Samet run from multiple piers in Ban Phe — Nuan Thip pier goes to Hat Sai Kaew, Saphan Sri Ban Phe pier to Ao Wong Duen
U-Tapao Airport (30 min west of Rayong) has domestic and some international flights — useful for avoiding Bangkok traffic entirely
Bring cash to Koh Samet — ATMs exist on the island but there are few, and card acceptance outside hotels is limited
Rayong city itself is mainly a transit hub; most visitors head straight to Ban Phe or the beaches — the city has good overnight seafood restaurants near the river
